E0277.title: Trait Not Implemented
E0277.description: This error occurs when you try to use a type that doesn't implement a required trait. Think of traits like contracts - if a function expects a type to have certain capabilities (methods), but your type doesn't provide them, Rust won't let you use it.
commonCauses: Forgetting to derive or implement a trait on your struct or enum
Using a type in a generic context that doesn't meet the trait bounds
Missing trait imports from the Soroban SDK

E0425.title: Cannot Find Value
E0425.description: Rust can't find the variable, function, or constant you're trying to use. This usually means it's not in scope, hasn't been imported, or has a typo in the name.
commonCauses: Typo in the variable or function name
Missing import statement (use ...)
Variable declared in a different scope
Trying to use a Soroban SDK type without importing it

E0308.title: Type Mismatch
E0308.description: You're trying to use a value of one type where a different type is expected. Rust is strict about types - it won't automatically convert between them.
commonCauses: Returning the wrong type from a function
Passing wrong argument types to a function
Mixing up similar types like u32 and i32
Forgetting to convert between Soroban types (e.g., String vs &str)

E0382.title: Use of Moved Value
E0382.description: You're trying to use a value after it has been moved. In Rust, when you pass a value to a function or assign it to another variable, ownership transfers (moves) unless the type implements Copy.
commonCauses: Using a value after passing it to a function
Using a value after moving it into a closure
Trying to use a value twice in different contexts
Not cloning when you need multiple uses

E0599.title: Method Not Found
E0599.description: You're trying to call a method that doesn't exist on the type, or the trait that provides the method isn't in scope.
commonCauses: Typo in the method name
Missing trait import that provides the method
Calling a method on the wrong type
Method exists but requires a different receiver (self vs &self vs &mut self)

E0507.title: Cannot Move Out of Borrowed Content
E0507.description: You're trying to move a value out of a reference, which isn't allowed because you don't own the data - you only borrowed it.
commonCauses: Trying to take ownership of data from a reference
Moving fields out of a borrowed struct
Pattern matching on borrowed data without using ref

SOROBAN_STATE_LIMIT.title: Contract State Size Limit Exceeded
SOROBAN_STATE_LIMIT.description: Soroban contracts have a 64KB limit on the total size of contract state. This error means your contract is trying to store more data than allowed.
commonCauses: Storing large data structures in contract storage
Accumulating too many entries in a Map or Vec
Not cleaning up old data
Storing data that should be kept off-chain

SOROBAN_AUTH.title: Authorization Required
SOROBAN_AUTH.description: Soroban contracts need explicit authorization for sensitive operations. This error means you're trying to perform an action that requires the user's permission.
commonCauses: Missing require_auth() call
Calling require_auth() on the wrong address
Authorization not provided in the transaction
Trying to access another user's data without permission

SOROBAN_PANIC.title: Contract Panic
SOROBAN_PANIC.description: Your contract panicked during execution. Panics stop execution immediately and revert all changes. Use Result types for recoverable errors instead.
commonCauses: Using unwrap() on None or Err values
Array index out of bounds
Integer overflow in debug mode
Explicit panic!() calls

SOROBAN_OVERFLOW.title: Integer Overflow
SOROBAN_OVERFLOW.description: An arithmetic operation resulted in a value too large or too small for the integer type. Soroban contracts should use checked arithmetic to prevent this.
commonCauses: Adding or multiplying large numbers without checks
Subtracting a larger number from a smaller one (underflow)
Not using checked_add, checked_sub, etc.
Token amounts exceeding i128 limits
